coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal formation mechanism of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots: From sawdust to carbon nanomaterials with blue fluorescence

coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Shiyu
Zhang, Peipei
Xu, Li
Lian, Hailan
Xu, Changyan
coredata.dc:description: The conversion of lignocellulose into fluorescent lignocellulose-derived carbon dots (LCDs) via hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) has attracted significant attention. However, the degradation pathways of lignocellulose components, their contributions to LCDs formation, and the temporal evolution of LCDs’ formation remain unclear. Given that HTC conditions influence LCDs’ structure and fluorescence properties, an orthogonal design was applied to investigate these effects. Results show that HTC temperature and feedstock type are two key factors due to the diverse hydrolysis behaviors of components. A temperature of 190 °C effectively balances fluorescence clusters’ generation and consumption, and the softwood, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic, with its high hemicellulose and G-type lignin content, is an ideal feedstock. During HTC, LCDs are formed with a carbon core of conjugated cyclic structures from cross-linked, polymerized, and carbonized carbocyclic compounds, and a shell of polymer clusters from aliphatic hydrolysates. Here, hemicellulose and amorphous cellulose contribute to aliphatic and carbocyclic compounds, imparting hydrophilicity and fluorescence, while lignin supplies carbocyclic compounds essential for fluorescent carbon cores. As conjugation increased, the maximum fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of LCDs reached 3.4 %. This work offers a theoretical basis for feedstock selection and explains the temporal formation process of LCDs, which is of great significance for the high-value utilization of lignocellulosic biomass and the regulation of LCDs’ structures and optical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignocellulose-derived carbon dots
Formation of carbon dots
Hydrolysis of lignocellulose
Hydrothermal synthesis
Orthogonal design

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots
Effects of hydrothermal conditions on lignocellulose-derived carbon dots formation
The formation mechanism of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots
Hydrothermal process of Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust
A proposed mechanism of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots’ formation
Fluorescence properties of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic diagram of the hydrothermal process. Photograph of LCDs (B) and solid residues (LSRs) (C).

(A) Solid recovery of reaction residues, the contents of macromolecules (B) in feedstock and reactant solid residues at different reaction stages. The crystallinity (C), and the content of small molecules (D) in feedstock before and after reaction.
The FTIR spectra of feedstock before and after reaction.

The XPS results of feedstock before and after the reaction. XPS full-scan spectra (A) and their corresponding high-resolution spectra of C1s (B), and the relative content of C1s in different chemical states (C).
The relative content of the main components in the LCDs.

The FTIR spectra (A), UV–vis absorption spectra (B), the XPS full-scan spectra (C), and their corresponding high-resolution spectra of C1s (D) of the LCDs, and the relative content of C1s in different chemical states (E).
Schematic diagram of the hydrothermal conversion process of feedstock to LCDs.
(A) Photograph of LCDs in daylight and ultraviolet light, and their FQY (B).
Excitation-emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy of LCDs-2 (A), LCDs-4 (B), LCDs-6 (C), LCDs-8 (D), LCDs-10 (E), and LCDs-12 (F).
Main components of LCDs analyzed by GC–MS.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal formation mechanism of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots: From sawdust to carbon nanomaterials with blue fluorescence

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The conversion of lignocellulose into fluorescent lignocellulose-derived carbon dots (LCDs) via hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) has attracted significant attention. However, the degradation pathways of lignocellulose components, their contributions to LCDs formation, and the temporal evolution of LCDs’ formation remain unclear. Given that HTC conditions influence LCDs’ structure and fluorescence properties, an orthogonal design was applied to investigate these effects. Results show that HTC temperature and feedstock type are two key factors due to the diverse hydrolysis behaviors of components. A temperature of 190 °C effectively balances fluorescence clusters’ generation and consumption, and the softwood, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic, with its high hemicellulose and G-type lignin content, is an ideal feedstock. During HTC, LCDs are formed with a carbon core of conjugated cyclic structures from cross-linked, polymerized, and carbonized carbocyclic compounds, and a shell of polymer clusters from aliphatic hydrolysates. Here, hemicellulose and amorphous cellulose contribute to aliphatic and carbocyclic compounds, imparting hydrophilicity and fluorescence, while lignin supplies carbocyclic compounds essential for fluorescent carbon cores. As conjugation increased, the maximum fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of LCDs reached 3.4 %. This work offers a theoretical basis for feedstock selection and explains the temporal formation process of LCDs, which is of great significance for the high-value utilization of lignocellulosic biomass and the regulation of LCDs’ structures and optical properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: As an eco-friendly and prolific energy source produced by plants through unique photosynthesis, lignocellulose holds significant promise for the circular bioeconomy, making its high-value utilization a prominent research focus in recent years (Sun et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023). In this context, the selection of conversion methods plays a key role in the efficient utilization and development of lignocellulosic resources. Among the several conversion methods such as physical, chemical, biological, and physicochemical approaches (Cazier et al., 2024), hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) stands out as a promising technique for efficient lignocellulosic biomass deconstruction due to its advantages including ease of operation, environmental friendliness, and low corrosion risks (Tripathi et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2024; Cazier et al., 2024).

Unlike conventional solid and liquid products derived from HTC of lignocellulose, a novel water-soluble product with fluorescent properties in the liquid phase, known as lignocellulose-derived carbon dot, has attracted much attention (Zhao et al., 2022; Gan et al., 2023). Generally, carbon dots refer to carbon particles that are finely dispersed and have a size of less than 10 nm, which are coated with functional groups such as hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl, and exhibit fluorescence emission, outstanding photostability, and negligible cytotoxicity (Sun et al., 2006; Liu et al., 2024a). Specifically, the lignocellulose-derived carbon dots (LCDs) formed from lignocellulose via HTC process integrate the advantages of both carbon dots and lignocellulose, such as adjustable fluorescent properties and eco-friendliness, and thus have received attention and development in many fields, e.g., sensors (Yuan et al., 2024), information encryption (Liu et al., 2024a), and biomedical science (Singh et al., 2024).

To achieve efficient utilization of lignocellulose and enable the on-demand synthesis of LCDs, it is essential to understand the formation of LCDs from lignocellulose. As typical lignocellulosic materials, individual components (e.g., hemicellulose (Gao et al., 2023), cellulose (Liao et al., 2020), and lignin (Zhu et al., 2024)), and unseparated complex lignocellulose like sawdust (Qiu et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2021) have been employed as precursors for the LCDs’ synthesis. These studies revealed that the LCDs’ formation process involves hydrolysis of components, dehydration of hydrolysates followed by their polymerization, carbonization, and then surface functionalization (Gan et al., 2023; Fang et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024). Nevertheless, owning to the variable structures and components of lignocellulose, changes in HTC conditions, such as feedstock and HTC temperature, can affect the degradation behavior of compositions, leading to the formation of LCDs with diverse structures and optical properties (Song et al., 2022; Fang et al., 2024). Studies have shown that the higher content of non-structural sugars and hemicellulose in feedstock contributes to the production yield of LCDs (Qiu et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2021). It has also been established that there is an interaction of lignin and xylan in the HTC process of lignocellulose for LCDs synthesis (Zhao et al., 2022). However, challenges remain in understanding the contributions of other major lignocellulosic components to LCDs formation. Moreover, it has shown that insufficient temperature prevents effective precursor decomposition, limiting monomer formation and LCDs production, while excessive temperature accelerates decomposition, causing carbonization of both precursors and LCDs (Xu and Li, 2021; Song et al., 2022). However, the component consumption process of multi-composition feedstock at selected temperatures, as well as the evolution of LCDs structure, need to be further clarified.

This study aims to investigate the hydrothermal formation mechanism of LCDs by analyzing feedstock component consumption and the time-dependent structural evolution of LCDs. Specifically, considering the varieties of HTC conditions, an orthogonal design was implemented to systematically evaluate the influence and relative significance of four key factors: feedstock type, temperature, reaction time, and solid-to-liquid ratio. In terms of feedstock, the sawdust from six typical softwood and hardwood, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica, Cunninghamia lanceolata, Cupressus funebris, Eucalyptus nitens, Betula platyphylla Sukaczev, and Populus tomentosa, was selected as raw materials due to its widespread availability and abundance as industrial byproducts. Furthermore, the degradation paths of three major components, and the time-dependent formation pathways of LCDs were investigated by analyzing the composition and structural properties of samples collected at various reaction stages. This work provides a theoretical foundation for feedstock selection in LCDs preparation, and elucidates the time-dependent formation mechanisms of LCDs, offering significant insights into the high-value utilization of lignocellulosic biomass and the regulation of the LCDs’ structural and optical properties.
The materials used are detailed in the Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: LCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, dry sawdust was dispersed in purified water at a series of solid-to-liquid ratios (weight/volume, g/mL) through 15-minute stirring at 25 °C before HTC treatment in a 100 mL PTFE-lined stainless steel autoclave reactor. The reactor was heated at target temperature (160, 190, and 200 °C) for the reaction time (2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 h). After being cooled to room temperature naturally, the brown mixture underwent phase separation through centrifugation at 9000 rpm for 10 min followed by 0.22 μm membrane filtration. The resulting lignocellulose solid residues (labelled LSR-t, where t denotes reaction time) were obtained by washing separated solid phase products with purified water and then dried under vacuum at 105 °C overnight. LCDs (marked as LCDs-t, where t represents reaction time) were isolated from the liquid phase via ethyl acetate extraction. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

As a quantifiable metric, the fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) was considered as an evaluation indicator in orthogonal design to assess the fluorescence properties of LCDs. Given there were four factors with different levels, the L18 (6136) orthogonal array was chosen for the experiment design (Table S1 and Table S2 in the Supplementary materials).

The factors sorting and optimum conditions were obtained by calculating and comparing mean response values (i) and range values (R) after running tests. The higher the R-value, the more significant the effect of that factor on the output. By comparing the R values of different factors, it is easy to identify which factor has a greater impact on the evaluation indicator (Li et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2024).
The characterization of LSR-t and LCDs-t is detailed in the Supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of hydrothermal conditions on lignocellulose-derived carbon dots formation
ce:section.ce:para:
Orthogonal design, a statistically efficient and structured methodology, was implemented to assess the influence of four key factors including feedstock type, hydrothermal temperature, reaction time as well as solid-to-liquid ratio, and optimize parameters for hydrothermal synthesis of LCDs. The analysis of R values revealed the following ranking of factors, in descending order of their influence on the FQY of LCDs: hydrothermal temperature > feedstock type > solid-to-liquid ratio > reaction time (Table S3 in the Supplementary materials). According to the
Ki
‾
value, the optimum conditions were the feedstock of Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica, the hydrothermal temperature of 190 °C, the reaction time of 12 h, and a solid-to-liquid ratio of 0.01 (0.5 g feedstock/50 mL purified water).

It suggests that hydrothermal temperature has the greatest effect on the FQY value. The FQY value rose to a high point as temperature increased from 160 °C to 190 °C followed by a decrease of FQY with a continued rise of temperature to 220 °C, which means that the LCDs synthesized at 190 °C can convert absorbed photons into fluorescence to a greater extent (Fig. S1A in the Supplementary materials). Generally, the hydrothermal decomposition pathways of multi-component feedstock highly depend on HTC temperature, owing to the heterogeneous hydrolytic behaviors of their components (Lachos-Perez et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Specifically, with a temperature range from 180 °C to 230 °C, the hemicellulose is decomposed after the decomposition of unstable substances (Lachos-Perez et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2024). The amorphous cellulose and crystalline cellulose are decomposed at the temperature ranges of 200 ∼ 230 °C and 230 ∼ 280 °C, respectively (Wang et al., 2024). Afterwards, lignin is hydrolyzed dramatically above 260 °C. Elevated temperatures facilitate the participation of more lignocellulose components in hydrolysis, while the carbonization degree of LCDs also increases under this condition, which affects the fluorescence performance of the LCDs (Gao et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2016). According to research on HTC temperature, FQY depends largely on the number of fluorescent fragments, which are generated through the polymerization of small fluorescent molecules during the reaction but are gradually consumed during carbonization to the carbon core of LCDs (Zhang et al., 2016). The number of fluorescent fragments relies on the competition between their formation and consumption. When the reaction temperature is 160 °C, the hydrolysate from non-structural substances and hemicelluloses continue to form small fluorescent fragments and grow into large fluorescent fragments. As the reaction temperature rises from 160 °C to 190 °C, part of amorphous cellulose participates in the HTC process, generating additional hydrolysates that contribute to the formation of fluorescent fragments. During this stage, the formation rate of fluorescent fragments is faster than their consumption rate for carbon core growth, resulting in an increasing FQY (Gao et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2016). Excessive temperature (220 °C) allows the decomposition of more cellulose and part of lignin, as well as increases the carbonization degree of the products (Gao et al., 2021a; Song et al., 2022). This promotes the growth of LCDs’ carbon core, which consumes fluorescent fragments at a faster rate than their formation, leading to a decrease in FQY. Therefore, 190 °C is an appropriate hydrothermal temperature in this case.

The feedstock type of lignocellulosic biomass is the second important factor affecting the FQY value. Based on the origin trees and constituent cells, wood can be categorized botanically into two main types: hardwood and softwood (Ruffinatto et al., 2023). There are differences in the components, content, and structural characteristics among various kinds of wood, which are closely related to hydrothermal hydrolysis behavior (Song et al., 2022). The results show that the FQY value fluctuated with the wood types changing (Fig. S1B in the Supplementary materials). It also shows the FQY values of softwood including Cupressus funebris, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica and Cunninghamia lanceolata were higher than that of hardwood containing Eucalyptus nitens, Betula platyphylla Sukaczev and Populus tomentosa, which might be related to their structural and compositional characteristics. Softwood, with its lower basic density relative to hardwood, has a looser and more porous structure, which is more conducive to hydrothermal degradation (Ruffinatto et al., 2023). Additionally, it indicated that, comparing the component content of feedstock and corresponding reaction residues, the consumption of hemicellulose and cellulose was more pronounced in the softwood than in the hardwood, which is attributed not only to the higher content of cellulose and hemicellulose but also to its greater proportion of guaiacyl (G-type) lignin units in softwoods (Zhao et al., 2022; Wang and Deuss, 2023) (Fig. S1E in the Supplementary materials). It has been established that the G-type lignin units dominate the physicochemical properties and fluorescence performance of lignin-based carbon dots, as well as serving as growth sites for small molecules produced during hemicellulose degradation (Zhao et al., 2022). Hence, softwoods, with their higher proportion of G-type lignin units, exhibit greater advantages in forming LCDs with relatively higher FQYs compared to hardwoods (Wang and Deuss, 2023). Furthermore, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolica stands out among these softwoods for the formation of LCDs with relatively higher FQYs, as it contains a greater proportion of hemicelluloses that are more easily degraded.

Above all, due to the different hydrolysis behaviors of each component, hydrothermal temperature and feedstock type are important factors affecting the FQY of LCDs among the factors considered. Moreover, according to the results of orthogonal design experiments, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust was selected as the carbon source with the reaction conditions of 190 °C and a solid-to-liquid ratio of 0.01 (0.5 g feedstock/50 mL purified water) to explore the formation process of LCDs in the following work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The formation mechanism of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots
Hydrothermal process of Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust
ce:section.ce:para: To figure out the formation pathway of LCDs, samples for analysis were collected from 6 stages (2 h、4h、6h、8h、10 h、12 h) during the HTC processing Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust (Fig. 1 A, Table S4 in the Supplementary materials). There is a wide of carbonized products formed in the hydrothermal process, including solids, liquids, and very small amounts of gases (mainly CO2) (Xiao et al., 2012; Zhao et al., 2014; Lachos-Perez et al., 2022). As the amount of process water formed during this process is quite higher than that of CO2 (Lachos-Perez et al., 2022), only aqueous phase products and solid residues would be discussed next. With the increasing reaction time, the hydrothermal liquid parts got browner (Fig. 1B), and the solid fraction became darker at the same time (Fig. 1C). To understand the transformation that happened in the hydrothermal process of Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust, the composition of solid residues and their structure were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2 A, solid residue recovery decreased gradually with the reaction duration extending, which suggests that a longer reaction time promotes more consumption of reactants to some extent. To further understand the consumption of main components, the constituent contents of residues collected from different stages were carried out.

In terms of polymeric substances including cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin (Fig. 2B), the number of which decreases to varying degrees as the reaction time increases. Specifically, there was nearly 17 % mass loss of cellulose in the initial 2 h of HTC process, and the quality of cellulose was unchanged for the next 6 h. Then cellulose in the solid residues lost about 28 % and 27 % of mass during the HTC at 10 and 12 h, respectively. It is generally accepted that cellulose coexists in the amorphous and crystalline phases (Wang et al., 2022). Compared to crystalline cellulose, the cellulose in the amorphous region exhibits a more disordered physical structure, making it more likely to hydrolysis (Wang et al., 2024). To verify that the mass loss of cellulose here is related to its arrangement, the crystallinity of the residues from the different stages was calculated by an X-ray diffractograms (XRD) analysis (Fig. S2 in the Supplementary materials). As can be seen in Fig. 2C, the relative crystallinity increased from 60.5 % to 84.6 % after 2 h of hydrothermal reaction, which indicates that the amorphous cellulose underwent hydrothermal degradation, so that the number of the amorphous cellulose decreased, and the relative crystallinity of cellulose increased. Then during the increase of retention time from 2 h to 8 h, the crystallinity decreased very slightly, which implies that the intermolecular hydrogen bonds in crystalline cellulose were slowly broken, and the crystalline cellulose adjacent to the amorphous regions was very slowly transforming into amorphous cellulose (Wu et al., 2023). Then, the crystallinity value began to decrease significantly as the reaction progressed to 10 h, implying that the content of the crystalline cellulose was decreasing, which is attributed to the gradual transformation of the crystalline cellulose into the amorphous phase under a long-term hydrothermal reaction at high temperature and high pressure (Wu et al., 2023). Overall, the trend of crystallinity change indicates that the mass loss of cellulose is influenced by the parallel arrangement and tightness of the cellulose molecular chains, along with the degradation of amorphous cellulose.

For the hemicellulose part, Fig. 2B shows that there was nearly 75 % of mass loss in hemicellulose during the first 2-hour reaction, and the rest of hemicellulose was completely consumed within the next 6 h. Compared with cellulose, hemicellulose has a low polymerization degree and branched structure with acetyl groups and uronic acids, exhibiting low thermal and chemical stabilities (Wang et al., 2024; Xu and Li, 2021). Therefore, hemicellulose was first decomposed during this treatment at 190 °C. Unlike hemicellulose, lignin, with a cross-linked three-dimensional aromatic framework structure, has excellent thermal and chemical stability, which makes it difficult to be decomposed during a HTC process at 190 °C (Wang et al., 2024). This is confirmed by the yellow and green bars representing acid-insoluble lignin (AIL), and acid-soluble lignin (ASL) respectively in Fig. 2B. During the initial 2-hour reaction period, AIL and ASL experienced nearly 22 % mass loss in total, likely resulting from the decomposition of lignin linked to hemicellulose or amorphous cellulose, as these structures are more easily degraded in the early phase of the reaction (Zhuang et al., 2019).

As far as small molecules are concerned (only organic matter is considered here), the mass of organic extracts decreased slightly with the increase of reaction time (Fig. 2D, blue-green bar), which might be related to the water solubility of these organic matter, some of which can be dissolved in water and degraded at the beginning of the reaction, but the remaining part could not participate in the reaction due to their low water solubility (Roffael, 2016). Moreover, water-soluble extracts (Fig. 2D), including cold water extracts (green bars) and hot water extracts (orange bars), also experienced more than half of the mass loss within the initial 2-hour reaction. This phenomenon is primarily attributed to the dissolution and degradation of water-soluble constituents, such as polysaccharides, alkaloids, and tannins (Roffael, 2016). Upon prolonged reaction duration, the extract content stabilized within a narrow fluctuation range, indicating that the residual extractives had limited solubility and resistance to further decomposition (Roffael, 2016).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of reaction residues from several stages were obtained to further verify the above points. According to Fig. 3 , the typical absorption peaks attributed to function groups in cellulose decreased gradually in intensity, such as peaks centred at 2901 cm−1 (relax/contract vibration of CH), 1370 cm−1 (bending vibration of CH), 1314 cm−1 (bending vibration of OH), and 893 cm−1 (anomeric carbon), which reveals that the degradation of cellulose occurred with the extension of reaction time (Wu et al., 2021) (Table S5 in the Supplementary materials). Moreover, the absorption peak at the centre of 1731 cm−1 was closely related to the acetyl group from hemicellulose (Gao et al., 2021a), which was only clearly visible in the black (untreated feedstock) and red (after 2-hour reaction) spectra, indicating that hemicellulose was rapidly decomposed and consumed. In addition, the intensity of absorption peaks e.g., 1691 cm−1 (relax/contract vibration of CO), 1591 cm−1 (vibration of carbon skeleton in the benzene ring), and 1265 cm−1 ((relax/contract vibration of C-OH), corresponding to lignin-related groups (Liu et al., 2024a), gradually increased with the increase of reaction time, probably because the mass loss of lignin was less than that of cellulose and hemicellulose in HTC processes.

XPS is an effective means of analyzing the chemical state of an element. As shown in Fig. 4 A, there were two characteristic signal peaks located at 284.8 eV and 532.08 eV, which correspond to C1s and O1s signals, respectively (Gao et al., 2021a). As far as the binding state of C atoms in wood, it is generally agreed that it is divided into four categories, C1, C2, C3 and C4 (Bouafif et al., 2008; Gao et al., 2020) (Table S6 in the Supplementary materials). In this case, the peaks located at 284.8, 286.6, 288.3 and 290.2 eV represented C1, C2, C3 and C4, respectively (Fig. 4B). As depicted in Fig. 4C, the content of each C1s of residues increased or decreased to varying degrees with the extension of reaction time. Specifically, the content of C1, representing the amount of lignin, gradually increased with prolonged reaction time, whereas C2 and C4, representing cellulose and hemicellulose, respectively, showed a gradual decline in content (Bouafif et al., 2008). This indicates that, as the reaction time increased, cellulose and hemicellulose underwent more significant degradation compared to lignin in this process. C3, which represented these three major substances, showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing (Bouafif et al., 2008) (Table S6 in the Supplementary materials). This is consistent with the conclusions from FTIR analysis.

To visualize the changes that occur in the feedstock during this process, the microscopic morphologies of the residues were studied with SEM (Fig. S3 in the Supplementary materials). The results demonstrate that the untreated raw material exhibited a typical intact plant cell wall structure, characterized by tightly arranged fibers with a smooth surface (Qiu et al., 2022). As the reaction time increased, the fibre surface transitioned from smooth to rough and frizzy, indicating the degradation of amorphous cellulose, hemicellulose, and part of lignin during the hydrothermal treatment. As discussed above, as the reaction time is extended to 12 h, all hemicellulose, approximately 72 % of cellulose, and 33 % of lignin in the Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust were decomposed gradually during this treatment, which means that the components in the liquid phase included hydrolysates produced from the degradation of cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A proposed mechanism of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots’ formation
ce:section.ce:para: LCDs, generated via the hydrolysis of lignocellulosic constituents, subsequent dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and surface functionalization during HTC, are present in the liquid phase (Qiu et al., 2022; Gan et al., 2023; Fang et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024). Studies have shown that the aqueous phase from hydrothermal treatment usually contains sugar-derived compounds, and lignin-derived compounds (Xiao et al., 2012; Zhuang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2024). In detail, sugar-derived compounds include monosaccharides, aldehyde compounds, organic acids, cyclopentanone derives, furanone derives, and monocyclic aromatics (Xiao et al., 2012; Zhuang et al., 2019). Lignin-derived compounds contain aromatic oligomers, phenol compounds, as well as methoxylated benzenes (Lachos-Perez et al., 2022; Zhuang et al., 2019). To further explore the composition of LCDs, the main components of LCDs collected from six reaction stages were characterized by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) analysis (Gao et al., 2012; Ji et al., 2024). The GC–MS analysis results of six samples (LCDs-2 to LCDs-12) are summarized and listed in Table 1 , and the corresponding chromatograms are shown in the Supplementary materials (Fig. S4). The chemical compounds identified from LCDs are mainly acids, amides, phenols, aldehydes, alkenes, and esters (Table 1). According to structure, these components are mainly divided into aliphatic compounds and carbocyclic compounds. The aliphatic compounds originated from the cracking, recombination, and polymerization of feedstock hydrolysate, contained both hydrophobic alkyl chains and hydrophilic amide or carboxyl groups, which enables them to spontaneously form amphiphilic structures, and give rise to polymer clusters through further cross-linking, polymerization, and carbonization (Zhang et al., 2016; Xia et al., 2019). In this amphiphilic structure, the hydrophobic alkyl chains aggregate internally through hydrophobic interactions, while the hydrophilic amide and carboxyl groups orient themselves outward, forming hydrogen bonds with water molecules (Xia et al., 2024). At the same time, the carbocyclic compounds (e.g., 5-hydroxymethylfurfural, furfural, vanillin), derived from the hydrolysis of the components (cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin), contributed to the formation of the conjugated carbon core structure of LCDs, providing the fluorescence centre and serving as growth sites for polymer clusters (Zhang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2024a; Li et al., 2025).

Specifically, according to Fig. 5 , aliphatic and carbocyclic compounds are represented by blue and green, respectively. LCDs-2 was mainly composed of aliphatic compounds (compounds No.1 ∼ No.6, Table 1) and a trace amount of vanillin (0.63 %). In this stage, water changed from a polar to a semi-polar state, and produced the hydrogen ions (H+), acidic hydronium (H3O+) and basic hydroxide (OH−) ions, which broke the lignin-carbohydrate complex (LCC) linkages and glycosidic bonds, and then dissolved the feedstock to a certain extent (Shen et al., 2024; Sun et al., 2022). The dissolved amorphous cellulose and hemicellulose underwent gradual hydrolysis, transitioning from fragmented polysaccharides to oligosaccharides and monosaccharides (such as glucose and xylose) (Gan et al., 2023). These monosaccharides and their derivates were subsequently cleaved, recombined, and polymerized to form polymer chains (Xia et al., 2024). Additionally, the generation of vanillin also demonstrated the hydrolysis of lignin in this stage, which aligns with the conclusion from Fig. 2. During this process involving multiple simultaneous reactions, LCDs-2 were formed, characterized by the structure with the carbon core composed of low-conjugation structures and polymer clusters, and the shell made up of polymer chains (Li et al., 2025). As the reaction time extended to 8 h, the relative content of carbocyclic compounds in LCDs-4, LCDs-6, and LCDs-8 gradually increased, resulting in a higher degree of conjugation in the carbon cores of these LCDs. With the reaction time further extended, the relative content of carbocyclic compounds went down, such as furfural, 5-methyl-2-furancarboxaldehyde and 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (5-HMF), which might be attributed to the complete consumption of hydrolysable hemicellulose and amorphous cellulose in the feedstock. Meanwhile, the number of other carbocyclic compounds associated with lignin hydrolysis, such as vanillin, 2-methoxy-phenol, and 1-(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-2-propanone, decreased to a minimum when the reaction reached 10 h, followed by a subsequent increase. Although they accounted for only a small proportion (< 5 %), they also contributed to the formation of aromatic clusters for carbon core (Zhuang et al., 2019). Therefore, compared to the conjugation degree of carbon core in LCDs-8, those of LCDs-10 and LCDs-12 exhibited an initial decrease followed by an increase.

To further analyze the evolution pathways of the LCDs’ chemical structure, FTIR, XPS and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra of samples collected from different stages were carried out (Fig. 6 ). According to the FTIR spectra (Fig. 6A), the board bands centred at 3320 cm−1 and 1023 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the OH and CO groups (Li et al., 2024), respectively. Besides, there were four peaks located in the double bond area becoming stronger and sharper as the reaction went forward. The two peaks at 1770 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO, while the peaks located at 1594 cm−1 and 1515 cm−1 were related to the stretching vibrations of CC groups (Gao et al., 2021a). The increase in CC intensity was highly correlated with the enlargement of the conjugated domain of the product, indicating that the small molecules were gradually aromatized with the further progress of the reaction (Liu et al., 2024a). This speculation could be supported by UV–vis spectra and XPS studies. According to Fig. 6B, the two absorption peaks centred at around 226 and 280 nm were attributed to the π-π* transition of the CC sp2 conjugated domain in the carbon core (Gao et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2024). It is worth noting that the absorbance reached a maximum at the 8-hour stage with the reaction time increasing, and then subsequently decreased, which reveals the evolution of the sp2 conjugated domain’s content.

As shown in Fig. 6, an XPS survey was conducted to further determine the chemical compositions of these six samples. The significant peaks at about 284.8 and 532.2 eV correspond to C1s and O1s (Gao et al., 2021a), respectively (Fig. 6C). The high-resolution spectra of C1s were used to analyze the chemical state of carbon atoms, the deconvolution of C1s surveys suggest that there were four binding forms including CC (sp2 hybrid carbon, 284.1 eV), CC (sp3 hybrid carbon, 284.8 eV), OCO/CO (286.2 eV), and O–CO (288.5 eV) for all samples (Fig. 6D), but their proportions were different (Gao et al., 2023). The proportion of CC (sp2 hybrid carbon) gradually increased as the reaction progressed from 2 h to 8 h, which confirms the results from FTIR and UV–vis studies, revealing that more conjugated structures were produced (Liu et al., 2024a). This is because, during this period, hydrolyzed compounds were continuously undergoing further hydrolysis, isomerization, rearrangement, condensation, polymerization, and aromatization to form conjugated molecules, which would promote carbon nucleus growth and finally form LCDs through cross-linking and π π accumulation (Zhu et al., 2024). After 8 h, the amount of CC (sp2 hybrid carbon) decreased, while that of CC (sp3 hybrid carbon) increased, which suggests that the longer retention time would aggravate the carbonization, and the deacidification and dehydrogenation could also make the carbon atoms with conjugated structure into saturated carbon atoms (Qiu et al., 2022).

In a word, during this 12-hour hydrothermal reaction at 190 °C, LCDs were formed, featuring a carbon core composed of conjugated cyclic structures derived from the cross-linking, polymerization, and carbonization of carbocyclic compounds, and a shell consisting of polymer clusters formed from aliphatic compounds derived from hydrolysates. In this process, hemicellulose and amorphous cellulose contributed to the formation of both aliphatic and carbocyclic compounds, which endowed the LCDs with hydrophilicity and a fluorescence foundation. Lignin primarily provided carbocyclic compounds, playing a key role in the formation of the fluorescent carbon cores of the LCDs. Specifically, as the reaction progressed, the degree of hydrolysis intensified, resulting in the generation of more carbocyclic compounds that participated in the nucleation process. However, further extension of the reaction time increased the carbonization consumption of these compounds, causing the conjugation degree of the carbon core to initially increase and then decrease. The formation mechanisms of the LCDs from Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic sawdust are shown in Fig. 7 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties of lignocellulose-derived carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8 A, diluted aqueous solutions of LCDs-2 ∼ LCDs-12 were all light yellow under the daylight, while they emitted blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet radiation (λex = 365 nm), which illustrates the generation of LCDs. Furthermore, according to Fig. 8B, the FQYs of LCDs-2 and LCDs-4 were almost the same. As the reaction progressed to 8 h, the FQY gradually increased to a maximum of 3.4 %, followed by a slight decrease and then a minor increase. The trend in FQY was similar to the variation in carbocyclic compounds content shown in Fig. 5, which suggests that the fluorescence behavior of LCDs was associated with the carbon cores formed by carbocyclic compounds, indicating the fluorescence emission mechanism of the synergy between the carbon core state and the molecular state (Wang and Lu, 2022; Han et al., 2025).

Furthermore, the fluorescence emission properties of LCDs are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that all LCDs had excitation-dependent fluorescence emission behavior, which might be attributed to the evolution of molecular fluorophores (Han et al., 2025). Additionally, LCDs-2 had a single fluorescence emission centre corresponding to blue fluorescence emission, while others had two centres (blue and green), which owed to the difference in fluorescence origin in LCDs (Li et al., 2024; Han et al., 2025). Specifically, LCDs-2 contained the carbon core with low-conjugation structures derived from vanillin and polymer clusters, resulting in their fluorescence centres primarily originating from fluorophores generated through the cross-linking and polymerization of vanillin (Liu et al., 2024a). While carbon cores in LCDs-4 ∼ LCDs-12 were from the cross-linking and polymerization of not only vanillin but also furfural, 5-HMF as well as other carbocyclic compounds, which gave rise to new fluorescent molecular clusters with more conjugated π domains (Song et al., 2015; Garcia-Millan et al., 2023; Han et al., 2025). Therefore, the fluorescence emission properties of LCDs were mainly influenced by the carbon core structure. The composition of conjugated domains in carbon cores dictated the types of molecular fluorophores, while the degree of conjugation influenced the FQY.

LCDs consist of a carbon core with conjugated cyclic structures from cross-linked, polymerized, and carbonized carbocyclic compounds, and a shell of polymer clusters from aliphatic hydrolysates. Here, hemicellulose and amorphous cellulose contributes to aliphatic and carbocyclic compounds, providing hydrophilicity and fluorescence of LCDs, while lignin supplies carbocyclic compounds essential for fluorescent carbon cores. Due to diverse hydrolysis behaviors of components, HTC temperature and feedstock type critically influence LCDs’ structure and fluorescence. A temperature of 190 °C effectively balances fluorescence clusters’ generation and consumption, and the softwood, Pinus sylvestris var. mongolic, rich in hemicellulose and G-type lignin, is an ideal feedstock.

Shiyu Gao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Peipei Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Li Xu: Resources. Hailan Lian: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Changyan Xu: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No.KYCX21_0895), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No.BK20221335), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.32071703).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biortech.2025.132493.
